summary: I was born on June 17th, 1919, in El Paso, Texas. I moved to Northwest Indiana in 1936 looking for work after The Great Depression. I was drafted into the United States Army infantry on April 12th, 1941, and served in the 38th Infantry Division, specifically K Company of the 152nd Infantry. Throughout my service, I stayed with the same company and trained in places like Fort Benjamin Harrison and Camp Shelby before deploying overseas.

I was born in El Paso, Texas, in 1919. It was my hometown before I moved to Northwest Indiana in 1936 to find work after The Great Depression. Although I left El Paso, my roots there were important as I started my journey toward military service and a new life in the Midwest.

The Great Depression had a big impact on my life. After it ended, around 1936, I left El Paso to come to Northwest Indiana looking for work like many others across the country. It was a tough time, and I even spent time in the Civilian Conservation Corps camps before being drafted into the Army.

I was drafted into the United States Army infantry on April 12th, 1941. I served in the 38th Infantry Division, 152nd Infantry, K Company. My training took place at Fort Benjamin Harrison in Indianapolis and Camp Shelby, Mississippi, and I stayed with the same company throughout my service. The Army took me through various locations including Louisiana, Texas, Florida, Hawaii, New Guinea, and the Philippines during World War II.

I was part of the 38th Infantry Division, specifically in K Company of the 152nd Infantry. We trained extensively in Mississippi and participated in maneuvers in Louisiana, Texas, and Florida. The division was responsible for security duties in Hawaii before moving on to combat operations in New Guinea and the Philippines during the war.

After being drafted, I went to Fort Benjamin Harrison in Indianapolis for initial processing and swearing in. It was the starting point before I was sent to Camp Shelby, Mississippi, for my infantry training. Fort Benjamin Harrison was where my Army journey officially began.

Camp Shelby in Mississippi was where I received my infantry training. The conditions were hot and swampy, which made the training tough but effective. We also went on maneuvers in nearby states like Louisiana, Texas, and Florida, including amphibious training in Tallahassee, Florida.

During my training at Camp Shelby, we were sent along with the Indiana National Guard. They were part of the same training and maneuvers we participated in before heading overseas. The Indiana National Guard was a key part of our preparation for combat.

While training in Mississippi, we went on maneuvers to Louisiana. It was part of our preparation exercises before heading overseas. Louisiana was one of several southern states where we trained in different conditions to get ready for combat.

Texas was another state where we conducted maneuvers during our training period after Camp Shelby. Along with Louisiana and Florida, Texas provided terrain and conditions for us to practice infantry tactics. These maneuvers were crucial to our readiness before deployment.

In Florida, specifically near Tallahassee, we did amphibious training as part of our infantry preparation. This was an important part of our training to prepare for island assaults in the Pacific. Florida's environment helped us get ready for the challenges we would face overseas.

New Orleans was the port where we embarked for overseas deployment on December 30th, 1943. We loaded onto the troop ship SS Manta Ray there and began our journey to the Pacific. The city marked the last stop on U.S. soil before heading to Hawaii and beyond.

Our troop ship passed through the Panama Canal on the way from New Orleans to Hawaii. It took about 11 hours to get from New Orleans to Panama, and then we transited the canal as part of our voyage to the Pacific theater. The canal was a crucial shortcut for our troop movement.

We arrived in Hawaii after about 17 days at sea aboard the SS Manta Ray. We stayed there for about six months, performing guard duty and rotating around the island of Maui. Our division was responsible for security on the main island, and we relieved the 20th Infantry of the 6th Division during our time there.

After Hawaii, we sailed to New Guinea, arriving at Oro Bay on July 17th, 1944. There we began to rebuild our fighting strength with new troops and started patrolling the area. The Japanese had already left, so there was little action, but it was an important staging area before moving on to the Philippines.

The Philippines was where we fought during the war. After New Guinea, we moved to the Philippines, landing in Manila and operating in areas like Subic Bay. Our division was involved in combat there, and it was the final major theater of operations for us in the Pacific.

The SS Manta Ray was the troop ship we boarded in New Orleans on December 30th, 1943, for our journey to Hawaii. It was a former medicine liner converted for troop transport. The voyage took about 17 days, and I remember the food on the ship was the best I'd ever eaten.

We passed by Hickam Field and Pearl Harbor when we arrived in Hawaii. Although Hickam Field was an Air Force base, our division was responsible for security on the island. It was a significant landmark during our arrival and stay in Hawaii.

When we arrived in Hawaii, we relieved the 20th Infantry Regiment of the 6th Infantry Division. They had been performing security duties before us, and we took over their responsibilities on the island. This was part of our division's role in maintaining security in the Pacific.

The 6th Infantry Division was the unit whose 20th Infantry Regiment we relieved in Hawaii. They had been stationed there before us, and our division took over the security duties on the island. This transition was part of the broader troop rotations in the Pacific theater.

On our way to New Guinea, we stopped at the island of Funafuti on July 10th, 1944. It was part of our transit through the Pacific, and we used it as a staging point before continuing to New Guinea. The island was already secured by Allied forces when we arrived.

During our voyage to New Guinea, we passed by the New Hebrides. We did not land there but it was part of the route we took through the Pacific islands. The New Hebrides were under Allied control by then, so it was a safe passage.

We arrived at Oro Bay, New Guinea, on July 17th, 1944, where we unloaded troops and supplies. It took three days to unload everything, and it was there that we began to rebuild our fighting strength with new men. Oro Bay was an important logistics and staging area for our operations.

On December 1st, 1944, we boarded the cargo ship Austin Daly to sail for the island of Leyte in the Philippines. This was part of our movement closer to active combat zones. The ship carried us and our supplies as we prepared for operations in the Philippines.

While I was in the Army infantry, I remember talking to someone who was in the United States Air Force. We passed by Hickam Field in Hawaii, which was an Air Force base. Although I was infantry, the Air Force was part of the broader military effort in the Pacific theater.
